---
title: "Memory System Organization and Architecture"
subtitle: "Module 4 — BCSE205L"
author: "Justus Raju"
date: last-modified
---

## Outline

::: {.incremental}
- Memory systems hierarchy and characteristics
- Byte storage methods and memory cell concepts
- Design of scalable memory using RAM/ROM chips
- Construction of larger size memories
- **Memory interleaving**
- Memory interface address map
- Cache memory: principles and management techniques
- Types of caches, cache misses, mean access time evaluation
:::

::: {.notes}
This module explores the hierarchical memory organization, from registers to main memory and cache. Students learn to design memory systems using RAM and ROM chips, understand memory interleaving for performance, and study cache memory mapping techniques and replacement algorithms.
:::

# Memory Systems Hierarchy

## Memory Unit

::: {.incremental}
- Memory unit is an essential component of a digital computer — needed for storing programs and data
- Memory that communicates directly with the CPU is called **main memory**
- Only programs and data currently needed by the processor reside in main memory
- Devices that provide backup storage are called **auxiliary (secondary) memory**
- All other information is stored in auxiliary memory and transferred to main memory when needed
:::

## Memory Hierarchy

![](../images/memory-hierarchy.jpg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

::: {.notes}
The memory hierarchy is a pyramid — registers at the top (fastest, smallest, most expensive per bit), then cache, main memory, and secondary storage at the bottom (slowest, largest, cheapest per bit). The goal is to give the illusion of a large, fast memory at low cost.
:::

## Memory Characteristics

| Characteristic | Options |
|---|---|
| **Location** | CPU / Internal (main) / External (secondary) |
| **Capacity** | Word size (bytes) / Number of words (blocks) |
| **Unit of transfer** | Word / Block |
| **Access methods** | Sequential / Direct / Random / Associative |
| **Performance** | Access time / Cycle time / Transfer rate |
| **Physical type** | Semiconductor / Magnetic / Optical |
| **Volatility** | Volatile / Non-volatile |

## Access Methods

::: {.incremental}
- **Sequential access** — access in predetermined linear sequence (e.g., magnetic tape)
- **Random access** — any location accessed directly, independent of physical location (e.g., RAM, ROM)
- **Direct access** — combination of sequential and random; general access to vicinity, then sequential search (e.g., hard disk)
- **Associative access** — word retrieved based on portion of its contents, not address (e.g., cache)
:::

## Performance Parameters

::: {.definition}
**Access Time.** Time from the instant an address is presented to memory until data is stored or made available for use.
:::

::: {.definition}
**Memory Cycle Time.** Access time + time required before a second access can commence.
:::

::: {.definition}
**Transfer Rate.** Rate at which data can be transferred into or out of memory. For random access: $\frac{1}{\text{cycle time}}$. For non-random: $T_n = T_a + \frac{N}{R}$
:::

## Performance Parameters — Summary

![](../images/memory-performance-params.jpg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

# Byte Storage Methods

## Big Endian vs. Little Endian

- **MSB** (Most Significant Byte) — left-most byte, carries greatest numerical value
- **LSB** (Least Significant Byte) — right-most byte, least effect on value

. . .

- **Big Endian** — MSB placed at the byte with the **lowest address** (first byte stored first)
- **Little Endian** — LSB placed at the byte with the **lowest address** (last byte stored first)

## Byte Storage — Visual

![](../images/byte-storage-endian.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

## Example: `0xDEADBEEF`

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Big Endian

| Address | Value |
|---|---|
| Base + 0 | `DE` |
| Base + 1 | `AD` |
| Base + 2 | `BE` |
| Base + 3 | `EF` |
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Little Endian

| Address | Value |
|---|---|
| Base + 0 | `EF` |
| Base + 1 | `BE` |
| Base + 2 | `AD` |
| Base + 3 | `DE` |
:::
::::

## Endianness in Practice

| Endianness | Architectures |
|---|---|
| **Little Endian** | Intel x86, DEC (PDP-11, VAX, Alpha) |
| **Big Endian** | Sun SPARC, IBM 360/370, Motorola 68000/88000 |
| **Bi-Endian** | PowerPC, MIPS, Intel IA-64 |

::: {.notes}
Big Endian is the most common format in data networking — TCP, UDP, IPv4, and IPv6 all use big endian order to transmit data. Little Endian is dominant on microprocessors (Intel x86).
:::

# Memory Organization

## Memory Cell and Organization

::: {.incremental}
- Basic element = **memory cell**
- Properties: exhibits two stable states (binary 1 and 0), can be written into, can be read
- Physical arrangement of bits to form words
- Two types of organization:
  - **1-dimensional** — linear arrangement
  - **2-dimensional** — matrix arrangement (row × column)
:::

# Design of Scalable Memory

## RAM Chip Structure

![](../images/ram-chip-structure.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

::: {.incremental}
- **Address bus** — specifies the row (word) to access
- **Data bus** — bidirectional, carries data in/out
- **Chip Select (CS)** — enables the chip when active
- **Read/Write (R/W)** — controls operation mode
- **High impedance state** — output disconnected from bus when chip is not selected
:::

::: {.notes}
For a 128 × 8 RAM chip: 7 address lines ($2^7 = 128$ words), 8 data lines. The chip operates only when CS1 = 1 and CS2 = 0. When not enabled, data bus is in high-impedance state.
:::

## ROM Chip Structure

![](../images/rom-chip-structure.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

::: {.incremental}
- Organized externally similar to RAM, but data bus is **output only**
- No R/W signal needed — read-only by design
- ROM chips pack more bits per chip than RAM (cells occupy less space)
- Example: 512 × 8 ROM chip needs 9 address lines ($2^9 = 512$)
:::

## Construction of Larger Memories

Three cases for building $N' \times W'$ memory from $N \times W$ chips:

| Case | Condition | Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N' = N$, $W' > W$ | Increase word size (add columns) |
| 2 | $N' > N$, $W' = W$ | Increase number of words (add rows) |
| 3 | $N' > N$, $W' > W$ | Increase both rows and columns |

. . .

$$\text{Number of chips} = p \times q, \quad p = \frac{N'}{N}, \quad q = \frac{W'}{W}$$

# Memory Interleaving

## Motivation

::: {.incremental}
- Main memory is structured as a collection of **physically separate modules**
- Each module has its own **Address Buffer Register (ABR)** and **Data Buffer Register (DBR)**
- Memory access operations may proceed in **more than one module at the same time**
- The aggregate rate of transmission of words to and from memory can be **increased**
:::

::: {.notes}
The key insight is that main memory is relatively slower than the cache. By organizing memory into independent modules that can be accessed concurrently, we can hide the latency of individual memory accesses and increase effective bandwidth.
:::

## What is Memory Interleaving?

![](../images/memory-interleaving-concept.jpg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

## Memory Interleaving — Definition

::: {.definition}
**Memory Interleaving.** An abstraction technique designed to compensate for slow main memory by spreading memory addresses evenly across memory banks.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Divides memory into a number of **modules** such that **successive words** in the address space are placed in **different modules**
- To implement an interleaved structure, there must be $2^k$ modules (where $k$ bits of the address are used for module selection — **high-order** or **low-order** bits depending on the scheme)
- Main memory is **relatively slower** than cache — interleaving improves the **effective access time**
- Particularly useful when transferring a **block of data** to cache
:::

::: {.theorem}
**Key Principle.** With $M$-way interleaving, effective access time for sequential access $\approx \frac{\text{cycle time}}{M}$, reducing access time by a factor close to the number of memory banks.
:::

## High-Order Interleaving

![](../images/high-order-interleaving.jpg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

## High-Order Interleaving — How It Works

::: {.incremental}
- **MSBs** of the address select the memory chip (module)
- **LSBs** are sent as addresses within each chip
- Consecutive memory locations are accessed from the **same module**
- When a block of data is transferred to cache, **only one module** is involved
- Maximum data transfer rate is **limited by the memory cycle time**
:::

```
Address format (high-order):
┌──────────────────┬──────────────────────┐
│  Module Select   │  Address within      │
│  (high-order     │  module (low-order   │
│   k bits)        │   remaining bits)    │
└──────────────────┴──────────────────────┘
```

::: {.notes}
High-order interleaving partitions memory into contiguous blocks. Module 0 gets the first N/M addresses, Module 1 gets the next N/M addresses, and so on. This is like giving each module a "chapter" of the address space.
:::

## High-Order Interleaving — Applications

::: {.incremental}
- **Multi-programmed / multi-tasking systems** — each process or program can be assigned to a separate module, reducing contention between them
- **Fault isolation** — if one module fails, only data in that contiguous block is lost; other modules remain operational
- **Memory protection** — contiguous address blocks per module make it easier to enforce access boundaries between processes
- **Embedded systems** — where different memory regions (code, data, I/O) are mapped to physically separate chips
:::

::: {.notes}
High-order interleaving doesn't speed up sequential access, but it excels in scenarios where multiple independent entities (processes, devices) access different parts of memory simultaneously. Think of it as giving each tenant their own apartment (module) rather than sharing rooms.
:::

## Low-Order Interleaving

![](../images/low-order-interleaving.jpg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

## Low-Order Interleaving — How It Works

::: {.incremental}
- **LSBs** of the address select the memory bank (module)
- Consecutive memory addresses are in **different memory modules**
- **Parallel access** is possible — hence, faster
- Allows memory access at rates **much faster** than the cycle time of a single module
- Results in **higher average utilization** of the memory system
:::

```
Address format (low-order):
┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────┐
│  Address within      │  Module Select   │
│  module (high-order  │  (low-order      │
│   remaining bits)    │   k bits)        │
└──────────────────────┴──────────────────┘
```

::: {.notes}
Low-order interleaving distributes consecutive addresses across modules in round-robin fashion. Word 0 in Module 0, Word 1 in Module 1, ..., Word M-1 in Module M-1, then Word M back in Module 0, etc. This is the scheme that enables true parallelism for sequential accesses.
:::

## Low-Order Interleaving — Applications

::: {.incremental}
- **Cache line fills** — when a cache miss occurs, an entire block of consecutive words must be fetched; low-order spreads these across modules for parallel retrieval
- **Pipelined processors** — sequential instruction fetches hit different modules, keeping the pipeline fed without stalls
- **Vector / array processing** — scientific workloads that sweep through large arrays sequentially benefit directly from overlapped access
- **Modern DRAM systems** — dual-channel and quad-channel memory configurations use low-order interleaving to maximize bandwidth
:::

::: {.notes}
Low-order interleaving is the dominant scheme in modern systems precisely because most memory access patterns have strong spatial locality — programs tend to access consecutive addresses. Cache line fills, instruction fetches, and array traversals all benefit enormously from spreading consecutive addresses across banks.
:::

## Why Low-Order Is Faster — Overlapped Access

With 4-way low-order interleaving and cycle time $T$:

```
         Time ──────────────────────────────────►

Module 0 │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│          │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│
Module 1 │  │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│          │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│
Module 2 │    │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│          │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│
Module 3 │      │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│          │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│

Output   │ W0  │ W1  │ W2  │ W3  │ W4  │ W5  │ W6  │ W7
```

- Each module still takes full cycle time $T$ to complete
- But a new word is delivered every $T/4$ time units
- **Effective bandwidth** = $4 \times$ single module bandwidth

::: {.notes}
This is the key insight of low-order interleaving. Even though each module is slow, by staggering the start times we get pipelined access. While Module 0 is busy, we start Module 1, then Module 2, etc. By the time we come back to Module 0, it has finished its previous access.
:::

## Comparison: High vs. Low Order

| Feature | High-Order | Low-Order |
|---|---|---|
| Module selection | MSBs | LSBs |
| Consecutive addresses | Same module | Different modules |
| Sequential access parallelism | No — one module at a time | Yes — overlapped across modules |
| Sequential access speed | Limited by cycle time $T$ | Effective rate $\approx T/M$ |
| Block transfer to cache | Single module | Spread across modules |
| Address distribution | Contiguous blocks | Round-robin / interleaved |
| Multi-program parallelism | Yes — different programs in different modules | Yes |

## Example: 4-Way Interleaving

Consider a memory system with **4 modules** ($k = 2$ bits for module select):

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### High-Order

| Module 0 | Module 1 | Module 2 | Module 3 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Addr 0 | Addr 4 | Addr 8 | Addr 12 |
| Addr 1 | Addr 5 | Addr 9 | Addr 13 |
| Addr 2 | Addr 6 | Addr 10 | Addr 14 |
| Addr 3 | Addr 7 | Addr 11 | Addr 15 |
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Low-Order

| Module 0 | Module 1 | Module 2 | Module 3 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Addr 0 | Addr 1 | Addr 2 | Addr 3 |
| Addr 4 | Addr 5 | Addr 6 | Addr 7 |
| Addr 8 | Addr 9 | Addr 10 | Addr 11 |
| Addr 12 | Addr 13 | Addr 14 | Addr 15 |
:::
::::

## Worked Example: Locating Address $(1B)_{16}$

**Given:** 8 modules (M = 8, $k = 3$ bits), each with 4 words. Total = 32 words.

Address $(1B)_{16} = (00011011)_2 = 27_{10}$

. . .

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### High-Order

```
  00 011 011
  ── ─── ───
  │   │   └─ Word within module
  │   └───── (not used for select)
  └───────── Module = 00011 = 3
```

Module select = top 3 bits = **011** = Module 3

Word within module = 27 mod 4 = **3**

→ **Module 3, Word 3**
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Low-Order

```
  00 011 011
  ── ─── ───
  │   │   └─ Module = 011 = 3
  │   └───── Word within module
  └─────────
```

Module select = bottom 3 bits = **011** = Module 3

Word within module = 27 div 8 = **3**

→ **Module 3, Word 3**
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
This is from the textbook example (Problem 6e). Both schemes happen to give the same module here, but the word placement within modules differs in general. Walk through a few more addresses to show the difference — e.g., addresses 0, 1, 2, 3 all go to Module 0 in high-order but to Modules 0, 1, 2, 3 in low-order.
:::

## Interleaving with DRAM

![](../images/interleaving-dram.jpg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

::: {.incremental}
- Main memory is usually composed of **DRAM chips** grouped into **memory banks**
- The memory banks are **interleaved** — accesses to different banks proceed **in parallel**
- Interleaved layouts give **far better performance** than contiguous allocation for sequential access
- Modern **multi-channel memory** (dual/quad channel) is an evolution of interleaving
:::

::: {.notes}
In practice, DRAM interleaving is one of the most important techniques for improving memory bandwidth. Modern systems use multi-channel memory configurations which are an evolution of the interleaving concept.
:::

# Memory Interface Address Map

## Address Map Concept

::: {.incremental}
- Designer must calculate **how much memory** is required and assign it to RAM or ROM
- Interconnection established from knowledge of memory size and available chip types
- A **memory address map** is a pictorial representation of assigned address space for each chip
:::

## Chip Count Formula

$$\text{Required chips} = p \times q, \quad p = \frac{N'}{N}, \quad q = \frac{W'}{W}$$

- $x$ = number of address bits per chip ($N = 2^x$)
- $y$ = number of bits for selecting among $p$ rows of chips ($p = 2^y$)
- $z$ = number of bits for selecting memory type (RAM vs. ROM vs. interface)
- **Total address bits** = $x + y + z$

# Cache Memory

## Cache Memory — Overview

![](../images/cache-overview.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

## Cache Memory Principles

::: {.incremental}
- Special **very high-speed memory** used to speed up and synchronize with the CPU
- Acts as a **buffer between RAM and CPU**
- Holds **frequently requested data and instructions** so they are immediately available
- Reduces the **average time** to access data from main memory
- The cache stores **copies** of data from frequently used main memory locations
:::

::: {.notes}
The processor first looks in the cache for a byte of data. If found (cache hit), access is fast. If not (cache miss), the data is fetched from main memory and a copy is placed in the cache for future use.
:::

## Cache Memory — Advantages & Disadvantages

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Advantages

- **Faster** than main memory
- **Less access time** — close to register speed
- Bridges the speed gap between CPU and main memory
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Disadvantages

- **Expensive** — costs more per bit than DRAM
- **Limited capacity** — typically KB to a few MB
- Adds **hardware complexity** for management
:::
::::

## Types of Cache

::: {.incremental}
- **Primary (L1) Cache**
  - Built **onto the processor chip**
  - Access time similar to processor registers
  - Very small size (typically 32–64 KB)
  - Built using **Static RAM (SRAM)**
- **Secondary (L2) Cache**
  - External to the primary cache
  - Located between L1 cache and main memory
  - Larger than L1 (typically 256 KB – a few MB)
  - Also known as **external cache**
:::

## Locality of Reference

::: {.theorem}
**Locality Principle.** Programs tend to access a relatively small portion of address space at any given time. We can predict with reasonable accuracy what instructions and data a program will use in the near future based on its accesses in the recent past.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- **Temporal locality** — recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again soon
- **Spatial locality** — items near recently accessed items are likely to be accessed soon
:::

::: {.notes}
Locality is the fundamental reason why caches work. Without locality, caching would provide no benefit. Loops exhibit temporal locality (same instructions repeated). Array traversals exhibit spatial locality (consecutive memory locations).
:::

## Locality of Reference — Visual

![](../images/locality-of-reference.jpg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

## Cache Operation — Hit and Miss

![](../images/cache-principles.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

::: {.notes}
The processor checks the cache first. On a hit, data is returned quickly. On a miss, the block containing the requested word is fetched from main memory into the cache, and then the word is delivered to the processor.
:::

# Cache Memory Management Techniques

## Overview of Cache Management

Cache management involves four key decisions:

::: {.incremental}
1. **Block Placement** — where can a block be placed in cache?
   - Direct Mapping / Fully Associative / Set Associative
2. **Block Identification** — how do we find a block in cache?
   - Tag / Index / Offset
3. **Block Replacement** — which block to evict on a miss?
   - FIFO / LRU / Optimal
4. **Update Policies** — how to handle writes?
   - Write-through / Write-back / Write-around / Write-allocate
:::

# Block Placement — Mapping Techniques

## Mapping — Terminology

::: {.definition}
**Blocks and Lines.** In the context of main memory (MM) to cache memory (CM), the unit of transfer is called a **block** (in MM) or a **line** (in cache). Blocks and lines are the same size.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Main memory is divided into **blocks** of fixed size
- Cache is divided into **lines** (also called cache blocks) of the same size
- **Mapping** defines the rule for which cache line(s) a given MM block can occupy
:::

## Mapping — Basics

Consider mapping **64 words** of main memory onto **16 words** of cache:

::: {.incremental}
- Main memory: 64 words → **16 blocks** of 4 words each
- Cache memory: 16 words → **4 lines** of 4 words each
- PA bits needed: $2^6 = 64$ → **6 bits**
- Block identification: 16 blocks → **4 bits** (upper bits of PA)
- Word within block: 4 words → **2 bits** (lower bits of PA)
:::

. . .

```
Physical Address (6 bits):
┌────────────────┬──────────────┐
│  Block Number   │  Word Offset  │
│   (4 bits)      │   (2 bits)    │
└────────────────┴──────────────┘
```

## Three Mapping Techniques

![](../images/cache-mapping-techniques.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

::: {.notes}
Each technique offers a different trade-off between hardware complexity and conflict miss rate. Direct mapping is simplest but most restrictive. Fully associative is most flexible but most expensive. Set-associative is the practical middle ground used in modern processors.
:::

## Direct Mapping — Concept

::: {.definition}
**Direct Mapping.** Each block of main memory maps to **exactly one** specific cache line. This is a **many-to-one** relationship.
:::

$$\text{Cache line number} = (\text{MM Block Address}) \mod (\text{Number of cache lines})$$

::: {.incremental}
- Block 0, 4, 8, 12 → Line 0
- Block 1, 5, 9, 13 → Line 1
- Block 2, 6, 10, 14 → Line 2
- Block 3, 7, 11, 15 → Line 3
:::

::: {.notes}
If a line is already occupied by a different block when a new block needs to load, the old block is evicted — no choice. This is the simplest scheme to implement but causes the most conflict misses.
:::

## Direct Mapping — Address Format

```
┌───────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────┐
│   Tag      │  Line Number     │  Word Offset  │
│            │  (Index)         │              │
└───────────┴──────────────────┴──────────────┘
```

::: {.incremental}
- **Physical Address** bits: $N$ where MM size = $2^N$
- **Word Offset** (Y bits): line size = $2^Y$
- **Line Number / Index**: number of cache lines = $2^{\text{index bits}}$
- **Tag**: remaining bits = $N - \text{index bits} - Y$
:::

## Direct Mapping — How Tag Bits Work

::: {.incremental}
- Multiple MM blocks map to the **same cache line**
- The **tag** distinguishes which block currently occupies that line
- On access: compare the tag from the address with the tag stored in the cache line
- **Match** → cache hit; **No match** → cache miss
:::

::: {.notes}
Think of it like apartment numbers in a building. The index tells you which apartment (cache line) to check. The tag tells you which tenant (MM block) should be living there. If the tenant doesn't match, it's a miss.
:::

## Direct Mapping — Worked Example

**Given:** MM = 128 words, Cache = 32 words, Block size = 4 words

::: {.incremental}
- PA bits: $2^7 = 128$ → **7 bits**
- Number of blocks in MM: $128 / 4 = 32$ → block bits = **5 bits**
- Word offset: $2^2 = 4$ → **2 bits**
- Number of cache lines: $32 / 4 = 8$ → index bits = **3 bits**
- Tag bits: $7 - 3 - 2 =$ **2 bits**
:::

. . .

```
PA (7 bits):
┌──────────┬───────────────┬──────────────┐
│  Tag (2)  │  Index (3)    │  Offset (2)  │
└──────────┴───────────────┴──────────────┘
```

## Direct Mapping — Access Trace

**Given:** 4 cache lines, blocks 0–7 map as: line = block mod 4

Reference string: **0, 2, 4, 0, 6, 2, 0, 4**

| Step | Block | Line | Tag in cache? | Result | Action |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Empty | **Miss** | Load block 0 → line 0 |
| 2 | 2 | 2 | Empty | **Miss** | Load block 2 → line 2 |
| 3 | 4 | 0 | Block 0 ≠ 4 | **Miss** | Evict block 0, load block 4 → line 0 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | Block 4 ≠ 0 | **Miss** | Evict block 4, load block 0 → line 0 |
| 5 | 6 | 2 | Block 2 ≠ 6 | **Miss** | Evict block 2, load block 6 → line 2 |
| 6 | 2 | 2 | Block 6 ≠ 2 | **Miss** | Evict block 6, load block 2 → line 2 |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | Block 0 = 0 | **Hit** | — |
| 8 | 4 | 0 | Block 0 ≠ 4 | **Miss** | Evict block 0, load block 4 → line 0 |

Hit ratio = 1/8 = **12.5%** — conflict misses dominate!

::: {.notes}
This trace clearly shows the problem with direct mapping. Blocks 0 and 4 both map to line 0, so they keep evicting each other (thrashing). Similarly blocks 2 and 6 thrash on line 2. Lines 1 and 3 sit completely empty. This motivates associative and set-associative mapping.
:::

## Direct Mapping — Tag Directory Size

::: {.definition}
**Tag Directory Size** = Number of cache lines × (Tag bits + Valid bit + Dirty bit)
:::

**Example:** MM = 128 words, Cache = 32 words, Block size = 4 words

::: {.incremental}
- Tag bits = 2 (from earlier example)
- Number of cache lines = 8
- Valid bit = 1 per line, Dirty bit = 1 per line (if write-back)
- **Tag directory** = $8 \times (2 + 1 + 1) = 32$ bits = **4 bytes**
:::

::: {.notes}
The tag directory is the overhead — extra storage needed beyond the actual data in the cache. For direct mapping, this overhead is relatively small because tag bits are small. For fully associative, tags are larger so the directory is bigger.
:::

## Direct Mapping — Drawback

::: {.definition}
**Conflict Miss (Collision Miss).** Occurs when two or more frequently used blocks map to the **same** cache line, causing repeated evictions even though other cache lines may be empty.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Example: If a program alternates between Block 0 and Block 4, and both map to Line 0, every access is a miss
- Other cache lines (1, 2, 3) sit empty — wasted capacity
- This is the main motivation for **associative mapping**
:::

## Fully Associative Mapping — Concept

::: {.definition}
**Fully Associative Mapping.** A block of main memory can map to **any** cache line that is freely available. This is a **many-to-many** relationship.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- All cache lines are freely available for any block
- Eliminates conflict misses entirely
- When all lines are occupied, a **replacement algorithm** must choose which block to evict
- **Disadvantage:** must search **all** cache lines for every access → expensive comparison hardware
:::

## Fully Associative — Address Format

```
┌─────────────────────────┬──────────────┐
│         Tag              │  Word Offset  │
│                          │              │
└─────────────────────────┴──────────────┘
```

::: {.incremental}
- **No index bits** — block can go to any line
- **Tag** = all block address bits (larger than in direct mapping)
- **Word offset** = same as before (determined by block size)
- Tag bits = PA bits − offset bits
:::

## Fully Associative — Worked Example

**Given:** Cache = 16 KB, Block size = 256 bytes, MM = 128 KB

::: {.incremental}
- PA bits: $128\text{KB} = 2^{17}$ → **17 bits**
- Block offset: $256 = 2^8$ → **8 bits**
- Tag bits: $17 - 8 =$ **9 bits**
- Number of cache lines: $16\text{KB} / 256 = 64$ lines
- **Tag directory size** = $64 \times 9 = 576$ bits = **72 bytes**
:::

. . .

```
PA (17 bits):
┌──────────────────┬──────────────┐
│    Tag (9)        │  Offset (8)  │
└──────────────────┴──────────────┘
```

## Fully Associative — Access Trace (LRU)

**Same reference string:** 0, 2, 4, 0, 6, 2, 0, 4 — **4 cache lines**, fully associative, LRU replacement

| Step | Block | Cache State | Result | Action |
|:---:|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | 0 | [**0**, -, -, -] | **Miss** | Load 0 |
| 2 | 2 | [0, **2**, -, -] | **Miss** | Load 2 |
| 3 | 4 | [0, 2, **4**, -] | **Miss** | Load 4 |
| 4 | 0 | [**0**, 2, 4, -] | **Hit** | — (0 moves to MRU) |
| 5 | 6 | [0, 2, 4, **6**] | **Miss** | Load 6 |
| 6 | 2 | [0, **2**, 4, 6] | **Hit** | — (2 moves to MRU) |
| 7 | 0 | [**0**, 2, 4, 6] | **Hit** | — (0 moves to MRU) |
| 8 | 4 | [0, 2, **4**, 6] | **Hit** | — (4 moves to MRU) |

Hit ratio = 4/8 = **50%** — no conflict misses! (vs. 12.5% with direct mapping)

::: {.notes}
Compare this to the direct mapping trace with the same reference string: 1 hit vs 4 hits. Fully associative eliminates all conflict misses. Blocks 0 and 4 no longer fight over the same line — they simply use any free line. The cost is more complex hardware for tag comparison.
:::

## Set-Associative Mapping — Concept

::: {.definition}
**Set-Associative Mapping.** Cache lines are grouped into **sets**. A block maps to a specific **set** (like direct mapping), but within that set, it can go to **any line** (like associative mapping).
:::

$$\text{Cache set number} = (\text{MM Block Address}) \mod (\text{Number of sets})$$

::: {.incremental}
- **$k$-way set associative**: each set contains $k$ cache lines
- Number of sets = (Total cache lines) / $k$
- Combines the best of both approaches
:::

## Set-Associative — Address Format

```
┌───────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────┐
│   Tag      │  Set Number      │  Word Offset  │
│            │                  │              │
└───────────┴──────────────────┴──────────────┘
```

::: {.incremental}
- **Word offset**: block size = $2^Y$ → $Y$ bits
- **Set number**: number of sets = $2^S$ → $S$ bits
- **Tag**: remaining bits = PA − $S$ − $Y$
:::

## Set-Associative — Special Cases

::: {.theorem}
**Unifying Principle.** Direct mapping and fully associative mapping are special cases of set-associative mapping.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- If $k = 1$ → **1-way set associative ≡ Direct mapping**
  - Each "set" has 1 line → block has no choice of line
- If $k$ = total number of cache lines → **Fully associative mapping**
  - Only 1 set → block can go to any line in that single set
- Typical values: $k = 2, 4, 8, 16$
:::

## Set-Associative — Worked Example

**Given:** MM = 128 words, Cache = 32 words, Block size = 4 words, **2-way** set associative

::: {.incremental}
- PA bits: $2^7 = 128$ → **7 bits**
- Word offset: $2^2 = 4$ → **2 bits**
- Number of cache lines: $32 / 4 = 8$ lines
- Number of sets: $8 / 2 = 4$ sets → set bits = **2 bits**
- Tag bits: $7 - 2 - 2 =$ **3 bits**
:::

. . .

```
PA (7 bits):
┌──────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┐
│  Tag (3)  │  Set No (2)  │  Offset (2)  │
└──────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┘
```

## Set-Associative — Example (32-bit Processor)

**Given:** 32-bit processor, 16 KB 4-way set associative cache, line size = 4 × 32-bit words = 16 bytes

::: {.incremental}
- PA bits: **32 bits**
- Offset: $16 = 2^4$ → **4 bits** (but word-addressed → **2 bits** for 4 words)
- Number of lines: $16\text{KB} / 16\text{B} = 1024$ lines
- Number of sets: $1024 / 4 = 256$ sets → **8 bits**
- Tag bits: $32 - 8 - 2 =$ **22 bits**
:::

. . .

For address `ABCDE8F8`:  binary `...1110 1000 1111 1000`

Set number = bits [9:2] = `00111110` = **set 62**

## Set-Associative — Access Trace (2-Way, LRU)

**Same reference string:** 0, 2, 4, 0, 6, 2, 0, 4 — **2 sets** (2-way), set = block mod 2

| Step | Block | Set | Set State | Result | Action |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | S0:[**0**, -] S1:[-, -] | **Miss** | Load 0 → set 0 |
| 2 | 2 | 0 | S0:[0, **2**] S1:[-, -] | **Miss** | Load 2 → set 0 |
| 3 | 4 | 0 | S0:[**4**, 2] S1:[-, -] | **Miss** | Evict 0 (LRU), load 4 → set 0 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | S0:[4, **0**] S1:[-, -] | **Miss** | Evict 2 (LRU), load 0 → set 0 |
| 5 | 6 | 0 | S0:[**6**, 0] S1:[-, -] | **Miss** | Evict 4 (LRU), load 6 → set 0 |
| 6 | 2 | 0 | S0:[6, **2**] S1:[-, -] | **Miss** | Evict 0 (LRU), load 2 → set 0 |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | S0:[**0**, 2] S1:[-, -] | **Miss** | Evict 6 (LRU), load 0 → set 0 |
| 8 | 4 | 0 | S0:[0, **4**] S1:[-, -] | **Miss** | Evict 2 (LRU), load 4 → set 0 |

Hit ratio = 0/8 = **0%** — worse than direct mapping here!

::: {.notes}
This is a deliberately adversarial example. All 8 blocks are even-numbered and map to set 0, so set 1 is never used. With only 2 lines in set 0 but 5 distinct blocks competing, thrashing is severe. This shows that set-associative mapping doesn't always beat direct mapping — it depends on the access pattern. In practice, real programs have more varied access patterns and set-associative performs better. If we used 4-way (4 lines per set, 1 set), this becomes fully associative and gets 50% hits.
:::

## Set-Associative — Tag Directory Size

**Given:** MM = 128 words, Cache = 32 words, Block size = 4 words, 2-way

::: {.incremental}
- Tag bits = 3 (from earlier example)
- Number of cache lines = 8
- Valid bit = 1, Dirty bit = 1 per line
- **Tag directory** = $8 \times (3 + 1 + 1) = 40$ bits = **5 bytes**
:::

. . .

Compare across all three techniques (same memory configuration):

| Technique | Tag bits/line | Lines | Directory size |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Direct mapping | 2 | 8 | $8 \times 4 = 32$ bits |
| 2-way set associative | 3 | 8 | $8 \times 5 = 40$ bits |
| Fully associative | 5 | 8 | $8 \times 7 = 56$ bits |

::: {.notes}
As associativity increases, tag bits increase (because fewer index/set bits are needed), so the tag directory grows. This is part of the hardware cost trade-off. More associativity = fewer conflict misses but larger tag storage and more comparators.
:::

## All Three Mapping Techniques — Summary

| Feature | Direct | Set-Associative ($k$-way) | Fully Associative |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Placement** | 1 possible line | $k$ possible lines (within set) | Any line |
| **Relationship** | Many-to-one | Many-to-few | Many-to-many |
| **Index/Set bits** | $\log_2(\text{lines})$ | $\log_2(\text{sets})$ | 0 |
| **Tag bits** | Smallest | Medium | Largest |
| **Comparators** | 1 | $k$ per set | 1 per line |
| **Conflict misses** | Highest | Moderate | None |
| **Replacement needed?** | No | Yes (within set) | Yes |
| **Hardware cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Used in practice** | L1 instruction cache | L1/L2 data cache | TLBs, small caches |

# Types of Cache Misses

## Cache Performance Parameters

::: {.definition}
**Cache Hit.** The processor finds the requested data in the cache.
:::

::: {.definition}
**Cache Miss.** The requested data is **not** present in the cache. The block must be fetched from main memory.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- **Hit ratio** ($h$) = $\frac{\text{Number of hits}}{\text{Total number of references}}$
- **Miss ratio** ($m$) = $1 - h$
- **Miss penalty** = additional cycles required to serve the miss
  - **Latency** — time to retrieve the first word of the block
  - **Bandwidth** — time to retrieve the rest of the block
:::

## Three Types of Cache Misses

::: {.incremental}
- **Compulsory miss (cold miss)**
  - First-ever access to a block — the block has never been in cache
  - Unavoidable regardless of cache size or mapping technique
  - Can be reduced by **prefetching**

- **Conflict miss (collision / interference miss)**
  - Occurs when multiple blocks compete for the **same cache line or set**
  - Happens in direct mapping and set-associative mapping
  - Can be reduced by **increasing associativity**

- **Capacity miss**
  - Cache is simply **too small** to hold all the blocks needed by the program
  - Not caused by mapping technique — caused by cache size
  - Can be reduced by **increasing cache size**
:::

# Block Replacement Policies

## When is Replacement Needed?

::: {.incremental}
- Replacement is needed in **fully associative** and **set-associative** caches
- In **direct mapping**, there is no choice — the old block is always evicted
- When a miss occurs and the target line/set is full, a **replacement algorithm** decides which existing block to evict
:::

## FIFO (First In, First Out)

::: {.definition}
**FIFO.** Replace the block that has been in the cache the **longest**, regardless of how recently or frequently it was accessed.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Simple to implement — just maintain an arrival order queue
- Does not consider usage patterns
- Can evict frequently used blocks just because they arrived early
- May suffer from **Belady's anomaly** — increasing cache size can sometimes increase miss rate
:::

## FIFO — Trace Example

**Reference string:** 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — **3 cache lines**

| Step | Ref | Line 0 | Line 1 | Line 2 | Result | Evicted |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | **1** | - | - | Miss | — |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | **2** | - | Miss | — |
| 3 | 3 | 1 | 2 | **3** | Miss | — |
| 4 | 4 | **4** | 2 | 3 | Miss | 1 (oldest) |
| 5 | 1 | 4 | **1** | 3 | Miss | 2 (oldest) |
| 6 | 2 | 4 | 1 | **2** | Miss | 3 (oldest) |
| 7 | 5 | **5** | 1 | 2 | Miss | 4 (oldest) |
| 8 | 1 | 5 | **1** | 2 | **Hit** | — |
| 9 | 2 | 5 | 1 | **2** | **Hit** | — |
| 10 | 3 | **3** | 1 | 2 | Miss | 5 (oldest) |
| 11 | 4 | 3 | **4** | 2 | Miss | 1 (oldest) |
| 12 | 5 | 3 | 4 | **5** | Miss | 2 (oldest) |

Hits = 2, Misses = 10, **Hit ratio = 2/12 ≈ 16.7%**

## LRU (Least Recently Used)

::: {.definition}
**LRU.** Replace the block that has **not been used** for the longest period of time.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Based on **temporal locality** — recently used blocks are likely to be used again
- Better hit rate than FIFO in most workloads
- Requires tracking **access history** for each block (hardware overhead)
- Most commonly used replacement policy in practice
:::

## LRU — Trace Example

**Same reference string:** 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — **3 cache lines**

| Step | Ref | Line 0 | Line 1 | Line 2 | Result | Evicted |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | **1** | - | - | Miss | — |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | **2** | - | Miss | — |
| 3 | 3 | 1 | 2 | **3** | Miss | — |
| 4 | 4 | **4** | 2 | 3 | Miss | 1 (LRU) |
| 5 | 1 | 4 | **1** | 3 | Miss | 2 (LRU) |
| 6 | 2 | 4 | 1 | **2** | Miss | 3 (LRU) |
| 7 | 5 | **5** | 1 | 2 | Miss | 4 (LRU) |
| 8 | 1 | 5 | **1** | 2 | **Hit** | — |
| 9 | 2 | 5 | 1 | **2** | **Hit** | — |
| 10 | 3 | **3** | 1 | 2 | Miss | 5 (LRU) |
| 11 | 4 | 3 | **4** | 2 | Miss | 1 (LRU) |
| 12 | 5 | 3 | 4 | **5** | Miss | 2 (LRU) |

Hits = 2, Misses = 10, **Hit ratio = 2/12 ≈ 16.7%**

::: {.notes}
In this particular reference string, FIFO and LRU produce the same result. This happens because the access pattern doesn't revisit blocks in a way that distinguishes "oldest" from "least recently used." With different patterns, LRU typically outperforms FIFO. For example, if block 1 were accessed frequently, LRU would keep it while FIFO might evict it.
:::

## Optimal Algorithm

::: {.definition}
**Optimal (Belady's Algorithm).** Replace the block that will **not be used for the longest time** in the future.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Produces the **minimum possible miss rate** — theoretical best
- **Impossible to implement** in practice — requires knowledge of future accesses
- Used as a **benchmark** to evaluate other algorithms
- Useful in analysis to know how close FIFO/LRU come to the ideal
:::

## Optimal — Trace Example

**Same reference string:** 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — **3 cache lines**

| Step | Ref | Line 0 | Line 1 | Line 2 | Result | Evicted (reason) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| 1 | 1 | **1** | - | - | Miss | — |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | **2** | - | Miss | — |
| 3 | 3 | 1 | 2 | **3** | Miss | — |
| 4 | 4 | 1 | 2 | **4** | Miss | 3 (not used again until step 10) |
| 5 | 1 | **1** | 2 | 4 | **Hit** | — |
| 6 | 2 | 1 | **2** | 4 | **Hit** | — |
| 7 | 5 | 1 | 2 | **5** | Miss | 4 (used at step 11, farthest) |
| 8 | 1 | **1** | 2 | 5 | **Hit** | — |
| 9 | 2 | 1 | **2** | 5 | **Hit** | — |
| 10 | 3 | 1 | 2 | **3** | Miss | 5 (used at step 12, farthest) |
| 11 | 4 | 1 | 2 | **4** | Miss | 3 (not used again) |
| 12 | 5 | 1 | 2 | **5** | Miss | 4 (not used again) |

Hits = 4, Misses = 8, **Hit ratio = 4/12 ≈ 33.3%**

::: {.notes}
Optimal achieves 4 hits vs. 2 for FIFO/LRU on this string. The key difference is at step 4: Optimal looks ahead and evicts block 3 (not needed until step 10) instead of block 1 (needed at step 5). This "future knowledge" saves two misses at steps 5 and 6. This is why Optimal serves as the theoretical benchmark — it shows the best any algorithm can achieve.
:::

## Replacement Policies — Comparison

Using the reference string: **1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5** with 3 cache lines:

| Algorithm | Hits | Misses | Hit Ratio | Implementable? |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **FIFO** | 2 | 10 | 16.7% | Yes (simple queue) |
| **LRU** | 2 | 10 | 16.7% | Yes (access tracking) |
| **Optimal** | 4 | 8 | 33.3% | No (needs future knowledge) |

::: {.incremental}
- Optimal always gives the **best or equal** result — it's the theoretical lower bound on misses
- LRU is the **best practical** algorithm — approximates Optimal using past access patterns
- FIFO is the **simplest** to implement but can make poor eviction choices
:::

::: {.notes}
In practice, LRU is the most widely used. However, true LRU requires tracking access order for all lines, which becomes expensive for high associativity. Many processors use pseudo-LRU approximations instead.
:::

# Cache Update Policies

## Write Policies — Overview

::: {.definition}
**Update Policy.** Determines how the cache and main memory are kept consistent after a write operation.
:::

Two scenarios:

::: {.incremental}
- **Write hit** — the block being written to **is** in the cache
  - Write-through or Write-back
- **Write miss** — the block being written to is **not** in the cache
  - Write-around or Write-allocate
:::

## Write-Through (on Write Hit)

::: {.incremental}
- Write to **both cache and main memory simultaneously**
- Main memory is **always up to date** with the cache
- Simplest policy to implement
- **Disadvantage:** every write generates main memory traffic → slower
- Best when writes are **infrequent**
:::

```
CPU ──write──► Cache ──write──► Main Memory
               (updated)        (updated immediately)
```

## Write-Back / Copy-Back (on Write Hit)

::: {.incremental}
- Write to **cache only**; main memory is updated later — **on replacement** of that cache line
- A **dirty bit** tracks whether a line has been modified
- Fewer main memory writes → **higher performance**
- **Disadvantage:** main memory may contain **stale data** temporarily
- Used in most modern processors
:::

```
CPU ──write──► Cache           Main Memory
               (updated)        (updated on eviction)
                  │
               dirty bit = 1
```

## Write-Around (on Write Miss)

::: {.incremental}
- Write directly to **main memory only** — do **not** load the block into cache
- Avoids **cache pollution** — blocks that are written but not read don't waste cache space
- Useful when written data is **unlikely to be read soon**
- **Disadvantage:** if the data is read shortly after, it results in a miss
:::

```
CPU ──write──────────────────► Main Memory
               Cache            (updated)
               (not affected)
```

## Write-Allocate (on Write Miss)

::: {.incremental}
- Write to main memory **and** load the block into cache
- Exploits **spatial locality** — if you're writing to a block, you'll likely read/write nearby words soon
- Often paired with **write-back** policy
- **Disadvantage:** the cache load adds latency to the write operation
:::

```
CPU ──write──► Main Memory
                  │
               load block
                  │
                  ▼
               Cache
               (block loaded)
```

## Write Policies — Summary

| Policy | Trigger | Action | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Write-through** | Write hit | Update cache + MM simultaneously | Simple systems, I/O coherence |
| **Write-back** | Write hit | Update cache; MM on eviction | High-performance processors |
| **Write-around** | Write miss | Update MM only; skip cache | Streaming writes, DMA |
| **Write-allocate** | Write miss | Update MM + load block to cache | General purpose (with write-back) |

## Common Write Policy Pairings

In practice, write policies are used in **two standard combinations**:

. . .

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Write-Through + Write-Around

- On **hit**: update cache + MM immediately
- On **miss**: write to MM only, skip cache
- MM is **always consistent** with cache
- Simpler hardware, **no dirty bits** needed
- Used in: simple systems, I/O-heavy systems, L1 caches backed by L2
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Write-Back + Write-Allocate

- On **hit**: update cache only (dirty bit = 1)
- On **miss**: load block into cache, then write to cache
- MM updated only on **eviction**
- Fewer MM writes → **better performance**
- Used in: most modern L1/L2 data caches
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
Patterson & Hennessy emphasize these two pairings because they're the only ones that make logical sense. Write-through + write-allocate would be wasteful (why load a block if you're writing through to MM anyway?). Write-back + write-around would miss the spatial locality benefit (if you're going to keep data in cache on hits, you should also bring it in on misses).
:::

## Write Policy — Worked Example

**Scenario:** 4 cache lines (direct mapped), write-back + write-allocate

Operations: `Write A`, `Read B`, `Write A`, `Write C`, `Read A`

(A and C map to the same cache line)

| Step | Op | Cache State | Hit/Miss | Action |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Write A | Load A, write to cache | Miss | Allocate A, set dirty=1 |
| 2 | Read B | Load B (different line) | Miss | Allocate B, dirty=0 |
| 3 | Write A | A is in cache | **Hit** | Update cache, dirty=1 |
| 4 | Write C | C maps to A's line | Miss | Write-back A to MM, allocate C, dirty=1 |
| 5 | Read A | A's line has C | Miss | Write-back C to MM, allocate A, dirty=0 |

::: {.notes}
Step 4 is the key moment — since A is dirty, it must be written back to MM before C can take its place. Without write-back, this writeback wouldn't be needed (write-through would have kept MM up-to-date), but we'd have had more MM writes at steps 1 and 3.
:::

# Mean Memory Access Time (MMAT)

## Performance Metrics — Definitions

::: {.incremental}
- **Hit Ratio** ($h$): fraction of accesses found in cache
  $$h = \frac{\text{Number of cache hits}}{\text{Total memory references}}$$
- **Miss Ratio** ($m$): $m = 1 - h$
- $T_C$: cache access time
- $T_M$: main memory access time
- **MMAT**: average time per memory reference considering hits and misses
:::

## Look-Through Cache

::: {.definition}
**Look-Through.** The cache is checked **first**. On a miss, the access to main memory is started **after** the cache miss is detected.
:::

$$T_{\text{avg}} = T_C + (1 - h) \times T_M$$

```
CPU ──► Cache ──miss──► Main Memory
        check           (sequential)
        first
```

::: {.notes}
In look-through, every access pays the cache access time $T_C$. On a miss, you additionally pay the main memory time $T_M$. So the cache time is always incurred, and the miss penalty is added on top.
:::

## Look-Aside Cache

::: {.definition}
**Look-Aside.** The cache and main memory are accessed **in parallel**. If the cache hits, the main memory access is cancelled.
:::

$$T_{\text{avg}} = h \times T_C + (1 - h) \times T_M$$

```
CPU ──► Cache          (parallel)
   └──► Main Memory
```

::: {.notes}
In look-aside, on a hit you only pay $T_C$ (the memory access is cancelled). On a miss, you pay $T_M$ (the cache contributes nothing useful). So the average is a weighted sum based on hit ratio.
:::

## MMAT — Example 1

**Given:** $T_C = 20\text{ns}$, $T_M = 200\text{ns}$, $h = 0.9$

. . .

**Look-through:**

$$T_{\text{avg}} = 20 + (1 - 0.9) \times 200 = 20 + 20 = 40\text{ns}$$

. . .

**Look-aside:**

$$T_{\text{avg}} = 0.9 \times 20 + 0.1 \times 200 = 18 + 20 = 38\text{ns}$$

## MMAT — Example 2 (Mixed Read/Write)

**Given:** Write-back cache (look-through), $T_C = 30\text{ns}$, $T_M = 300\text{ns}$

- Read hit ratio = 80%, Write hit ratio = 90%
- Reads = 60% of references, Writes = 40%

. . .

**Read $T_{\text{avg}}$:** $30 + (1 - 0.8) \times 300 = 30 + 60 = 90\text{ns}$

**Write $T_{\text{avg}}$:** $30 + (1 - 0.9) \times 300 = 30 + 30 = 60\text{ns}$

. . .

**Overall $T_{\text{avg}}$:** $0.6 \times 90 + 0.4 \times 60 = 54 + 24 = 78\text{ns}$

## MMAT — Example 2 (Look-Aside Variant)

**Same problem** but with **look-aside** cache:

. . .

**Read $T_{\text{avg}}$:** $0.8 \times 30 + 0.2 \times 300 = 24 + 60 = 84\text{ns}$

**Write $T_{\text{avg}}$:** $0.9 \times 30 + 0.1 \times 300 = 27 + 30 = 57\text{ns}$

. . .

**Overall $T_{\text{avg}}$:** $0.6 \times 84 + 0.4 \times 57 = 50.4 + 22.8 = 73.2\text{ns}$
